subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
Nidavellir includes:

- **3 tavern boards** (Laughing Goblin, Dancing Dragon, Shining Horse)
- **73 dwarf cards** in 5 classes: Blacksmith, Hunter, Explorer, Miner, Warrior
- **18 hero cards** (powerful unique recruits)
- **5 distinction cards** (end-game bonuses for class majorities)
- **Coin tokens** in values from 0 to 25+
- **5 player gem tokens** (one per player)
- **Exchange board** for coin upgrades
- **Coin purses** (player boards to hold 5 coins)
- **Royal treasure tiles**
- **Score pad**

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Place the **3 tavern boards** side by side in the center.
2. Separate dwarf cards into **Age 1** and **Age 2** decks. Shuffle each separately.
3. Deal dwarf cards face up to each tavern based on player count (typically **3 cards per tavern** in a 4-player game, adjusted for other counts).
4. Place the **hero cards** face up in a row near the taverns.
5. Set up the **exchange board** with coin tokens organized by value.
6. Place **distinction cards** near the play area.
7. The game plays **8 rounds** (4 rounds of Age 1, then 4 rounds of Age 2).

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
1. Each player takes a **coin purse** (player board) that holds 5 coins.
2. Each player starts with coins of value: **0, 2, 3, 4, and 5**. These are placed in the 5 slots of the purse.
3. Each player takes a **gem token** of their color.
4. Place your gem on the turn order track.
5. You begin with **no recruited dwarves** — your army builds over the game.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
Nidavellir is a **blind bidding and set collection** game set in Norse mythology. As an Elvaland, you recruit dwarves from taverns by bidding coins, building an army to fight the dragon Fafnir.

The unique twist: your **coins grow in value** during the game. When you bid your lowest coin at a tavern, you can **upgrade** it by adding another coin's value to it. Over the game, your 0-value coin can grow into a 15+ value coin.

Recruit sets of **5 different dwarf classes** to unlock powerful **heroes**. The player with the most **bravery points** (from dwarves, heroes, and distinctions) wins.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each round follows these steps:

### 1. Place Bids (Simultaneous)
All players simultaneously place **one coin face down** on each of the **3 tavern boards**. You assign 3 of your 5 coins to the 3 taverns (the other 2 remain in your purse).

### 2. Reveal and Resolve Taverns (One at a Time)
For each tavern, in order:
1. All players **reveal their bid coin** simultaneously.
2. The player with the **highest bid** picks first, choosing one dwarf card from that tavern.
3. Second highest picks next, and so on.
4. **Ties** are broken by the gem order track (whoever is higher on the track goes first; that player then moves to the bottom of the track).

### 3. Coin Upgrade
After all 3 taverns resolve, check each player: the coin that was your **lowest bid** (or the leftover coin if tied) gets upgraded:
- **Take that coin** and add the value of your other non-bid coin in your purse to it.
- Replace the upgraded coin with a **new coin of the combined value** from the exchange board.
- Example: If your 0-coin was lowest and your remaining purse coin is 5, your 0-coin becomes a **5-coin**.

### 4. Refill Taverns
Deal new dwarf cards to refill each tavern for the next round.

subtopics.title: Key Actions
subtopics.content:
### Recruiting Dwarves
Each dwarf card belongs to one of **5 classes**, each with a unique scoring mechanism:
- **Warriors** (red): Score bravery points shown on their card. More warriors = more raw points.
- **Hunters** (green): Score based on **how many hunters** you have (escalating: 1=1, 2=3, 3=6, 4=10, 5=15, etc.).
- **Miners** (blue): Score based on **the highest single miner value** in your collection (not sum).
- **Explorers** (teal): Score based on **the sum** of all explorer values.
- **Blacksmiths** (purple): Score based on **how many blacksmiths** you have (escalating scale like hunters but different values).

### Recruiting Heroes
Whenever you have **at least one dwarf of each of the 5 classes** that hasn't already triggered a hero, you immediately recruit a **hero** from the hero row. Heroes are powerful — they count as a class of your choice and have high bravery values or special abilities.

### Coin Upgrading
The coin upgrade is the engine of the game:
- Your **lowest bid coin** is combined with your **remaining purse coin**.
- This means bidding low at one tavern is actually beneficial — that low coin gets upgraded.
- Strategic bidding involves balancing winning dwarves vs. growing your coin values.

### Distinction Cards
At the end of Age 1, **distinction cards** are awarded to the player with the most dwarves of each class. Each distinction grants a one-time bonus or ongoing ability.

subtopics.title: Special Rules
subtopics.content:
### Age Transition
After **4 rounds** of Age 1:
1. Award **distinction cards** to players with the most dwarves of each class.
2. Remove remaining Age 1 cards.
3. Begin dealing from the **Age 2 deck** (stronger dwarves).

### The 0-Coin Strategy
Your starting 0-coin is crucial. Since it's always your lowest, it gets upgraded first. By strategically pairing it with high remaining purse coins, you can rapidly inflate its value.

### Gem Track (Tiebreaking)
The gem track determines who wins bid ties. After using your priority, your gem drops to the bottom. Managing your position on this track is a subtle strategic element.

### Royal Treasure
Some heroes grant **royal treasure tiles** which provide end-game scoring bonuses.

### Hero Timing
Heroes are recruited **immediately** when you complete a set of 5 classes. You choose which hero to take. Heroes then count as a class of your choice, potentially triggering another hero immediately if you have another complete set.

### Camp Tiles (Expansion)
The expansion adds a camp area where players who didn't win a dwarf at a tavern can gain alternative benefits.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
The game ends after **8 rounds** (4 Age 1 + 4 Age 2). Calculate final bravery points:

### Class Scoring
1. **Warriors**: Sum of all bravery values on warrior cards.
2. **Hunters**: Score from a triangular number table based on count (1→1, 2→3, 3→6, 4→10, 5→15, 6→21...).
3. **Miners**: Score only the **single highest-value** miner card.
4. **Explorers**: Sum of all bravery values on explorer cards.
5. **Blacksmiths**: Score from an escalating table based on count.

### Hero Points
6. Add bravery values from all **hero cards**.

### Distinction Bonuses
7. Add any **distinction card** bonuses.

### Coin Value
8. Add the value of your **highest coin** to your score.

### Winning
The player with the **highest total bravery points** wins. Tiebreaker: highest single coin value.

subtopics.title: Beginner Tips
subtopics.content:
- **Upgrade your 0-coin early**: Always ensure your 0-coin gets upgraded in the first few rounds. It snowballs in value.
- **Complete sets of 5**: Heroes are extremely powerful. Getting one dwarf of each class quickly is critical.
- **Don't overbid**: Winning a tavern with your highest coin means that coin doesn't get upgraded. Balance winning vs. growing.
- **Watch opponents' collections**: Know which classes they're pursuing and compete or pivot accordingly.
- **Miners are deceptive**: You only score the highest single miner, so one high-value miner is better than many low ones.

subtopics.title: Key Strategies
subtopics.content:
- **Coin engine priority**: In early rounds, focus on upgrading coins rather than winning specific dwarves. High coins in late rounds give you first pick when it matters most.
- **Hunter/Blacksmith stacking**: These classes scale exponentially with count. Collecting 6+ hunters can outscore everything else.
- **Hero chain**: If you recruit a hero that counts as a needed class, it may immediately complete another set, chaining into a second hero in one turn.
- **Distinction control**: Having the most of a class at the end of Age 1 grants powerful distinctions. The Warrior distinction in particular can swing the game.
- **Bluff bidding**: Place medium coins where you don't care and your high coin where you do. Opponents can't see your bids until revealed.
- **Miner efficiency**: Draft one high-value miner and ignore the rest. That single card scores as much as collecting many.
- **Late-game coin scoring**: Your highest coin scores directly as points. A 20+ coin is worth as much as several dwarves.
